focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 8

The shower ran at 6:15. Mira knew it was 6:15 because she had made it 6:15, had claimed that slot in the first week after the classification meeting by simply being first, by stepping into the bathroom with her towel while Dan was still blinking on the couch in his boxers. Now it was law. Not negotiated, not discussed — absorbed into the apartment's operating rhythm, another constant, like the refrigerator's forty-minute click or the creak on the second landing that they both stepped over when Theo was sleeping. The apartment ran on protocols neither of them had written down, and Mira, who had spent her professional life writing such things down, found this both efficient and quietly maddening.

She stood under the water and listened to the building's pipes groan. Third floor of a Park Slope brownstone, and the plumbing sounded like it remembered the Carter administration. The water pressure was adequate if no one flushed a toilet on the second floor, which Mrs. Okafor sometimes did at exactly 6:20, introducing a spike of cold that Mira had learned to anticipate by shifting her body sideways in the stall, letting the icy burst hit the tile instead of her spine. She had mapped this. She had mapped the building's water system — every pipe, every pressure drop, every forty-minute cycle — because mapping was what she did when she couldn't change anything, and she couldn't change the plumbing any more than she could change the infrastructure classification that kept her in this apartment with a man she'd asked to stop being married to.

The bathroom was small. When Dan used it at 6:45 — his assigned window, assigned by her default, accepted by his silence — she could hear him through the wall if she was in the bedroom. The specific sounds of him: the cabinet doo...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...abit, organizing the physical world when the larger situation refused to be organized. Through the doorway she could hear Dan breathing on the couch, the slow, even sound of someone who fell asleep watching something and would wake at three with a stiff neck and the blue light of a paused screen on his face. The refrigerator clicked. She counted without deciding to — right on schedule, forty minutes since the last one. The building settled around her, the old brownstone making its small nocturnal adjustments, the second-floor landing creaking under weight or weather or nothing.

All of it was classified. All of it was infrastructure. The breathing and the clicking and the creaking — all of it itemized in a document she'd read three times on the train, all of it assigned a dependency coefficient, all of it part of a node that had just been revised and not changed.

She sat at the table in the dark, in the apartment that was not hers to leave, in the marriage that was not hers to end, and she didn't cry and she didn't plan. She didn't open the laptop and look for another angle, because there were no more angles. For the first time in her adult life, Mira's own competence had shown her exactly the shape of a problem she could not solve, and the problem's shape was the shape of everything — the system, the classification, the apartment, the marriage, the refrigerator, the sleeping man on the couch, the son in the next room, the city outside the window that adjusted its streetlights for her and didn't care if she was crying or walking or leaving or staying, because it was locally optimal, all of it, every last coefficient and dependency and bilateral node and the woman sitting alone at a kitchen table at midnight who had run out of strategies and didn't know what to do next.
